sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=48, col=39).
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 19, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 11 of about 33 target actions so far.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 11 actions returned the board to a state already seen 11 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 11 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the current state, the white diamond is at [[4, 17], [6, 19], [8, 17], [6, 15]] which is the starting position. The game is still in GAME_OVER state but the level hasn't reset yet.
Looking at the key objects:
- Orange circle at [10, 23] (20 pixels)
- Purple square at [39, 48] (20 pixels)
- Orange ring pixels at bottom: [48, 39], [48, 41], [49, 38], [49, 42], [50, 37], [50, 43], [52, 37], [52, 43], [53, 38], [53, 42], [54, 39], [54, 41]
- Purple ring pixels at top right: [15, 56], [15, 58], [16, 55], [16, 59], [17, 54], [17, 60], [19, 54], [19, 60], [20, 55], [20, 59], [21, 56], [21, 58]
- Magenta pixels at [12, 24] and [41, 49] (centers of orange circle and purple square)
- Orange single pixels at [6, 17], [9, 49], [21, 8], [48, 39] (gray diamond positions and one orange ring pixel)
Wait, I see "color=O, pixels=1, pos=[48, 39]" which is one of the orange ring pixels. This is interesting - it seems like the orange ring pixel at [48, 39] is still there, but the white diamond moved away from it.
Actually, looking more carefully, the orange ring has 12 pixels, and I see 12 orange single pixels listed: [48, 39], [48, 41], [49, 38], [49, 42], [50, 37], [50, 43], [52, 37], [52, 43], [53, 38], [53, 42], [54, 39], [54, 41]. Plus there are orange pixels at [6, 17], [9, 49], [21, 8] which are the gray diamond positions (now showing as orange after the white diamond visited them).
So the level is still in GAME_OVER state. The white diamond is at the starting position. I need to wait for the level to reset.
Actually, looking at the valid_actions, it shows ["MOUSE"], which means I can still take actions. But the game is in GAME_OVER state. Let me try taking an action to see if the level resets.
Wait, I realize the issue. The game is in GAME_OVER state, but the level hasn't reset yet. The system says "the runner will auto-reset before the next analyzer turn." This means the level will reset when I make my next move (in the next turn).
But I'm still in the same turn, and the game is over. I should not take any more actions in this turn. I'll wait for the next turn when the level resets.
Actually, looking at the message "You are still on the same level" and "The game is over", it seems like the level is stuck in GAME_OVER state. The valid_actions shows ["MOUSE"], but trying to take an action might not work.
Let me try taking an action to see if it triggers a reset or if it fails.
Actually, I think the issue is that I'm in a loop where the game is over but the level hasn't reset. The system keeps showing me the same state. I need to wait for the next analyzer turn when the level will auto-reset.
Since I can't take meaningful actions in the GAME_OVER state, I should just wait for the next turn. But the system is asking me to take actions. Let me try taking an action to see what happens.
Actually, looking at the last_action_result, it shows "executed": False and "stop_reason": "previous_game_over". This means the action was not executed because the game was already over. So I can't take any more actions in this state.
I need to wait for the next turn when the level resets. But the system keeps asking me to take actions. This is a problem.
Let me just try to take an action and see if it triggers a reset.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The game is over. Let me try to take an action to see if it triggers a reset.
# First, let me understand what happened.
# When I clicked on orange ring pixel, the white diamond moved there and game ended.
# This suggests clicking on the wrong ring causes game over.
# In Level 1, I clicked on purple ring (matching the purple square color) and it worked.
# For Level 2, maybe I need to click on the PURPLE ring (matching the purple square color), not the orange ring.
# But the game is over, so I need to wait for reset.
# Let me check if I can take any action.
print(f"Last action result: {last_action_result}")
print(f"State: {last_action_result.get('state', 'N/A')}")

# Try a safe action to see if level resets
try:
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 6, 'col': 17}])
    print(f"After action - Level: {current_frame.level}, Step: {current_frame.step}")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Action failed: {e}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
